goal: Start with 1–2 simple, high-impact habits and test for one week.
constraints: Regimen must be simple, sustainable and easy to keep.
Planning time should be brief: daily 5 minutes end-of-day plus a focused weekly session (20–60 minutes).
Hybrid work schedule: office ~3 days/week and WFH ~2 days/week.
Office days include a 40-minute rush-hour commute each way; build buffers and adjust timing accordingly.
Two evening language classes per week; those evenings reserved; no chores or heavy work those evenings.
Sleep target: ~7 hours per night; avoid extremely early starts; wake time aligned to sleep target.
Pickup window: after-school clubs ending at 5:30 PM require availability for pickup; block 5:00–6:00 PM as a buffer on those days; treat pickup as a non-negotiable appointment.
Preference for simple tools: prefer paper lists or minimal phone setups over complex apps.
decisions: Recommend selecting 1–2 habits from the MITs list to trial for one week.
Evaluate implementing shared family calendar and weekly family sync.
Await schedule details (wake times, office days, meeting load) to draft a hybrid-friendly plan.
Adopt simple MIT delivery using paper card or a single phone note as the primary method.

Pause before accepting - dont say yes immediately; ask for time to review your schedule.
Ask for outcome and deadline to understand real need before committing.
Offer an alternative: delegate, delay or do a smaller slice.
Protect blocks: treat calendar blocks as commitments and push requests to later times.
Triage quickly: Do (<=10 min) / Defer / Delegate / Decline.
Be brief and solution-oriented: provide a next step or trade-off with your no.
Use boundaries consistently: set office hours or designated response windows for non-urgent asks.
Need time to check calendar: can I confirm after I check my schedule? I'll reply by [time].
Ask about urgency/outcome: what's the required outcome and deadline so I can prioritize it?
Offer an alternative time: I'm booked until [time]; can we move this to [two options]?
Offer a partial help or delegate: I can handle the first 30 minutes or connect you with someone who can finish.
Push back with trade-off: I can take this on by [date], but I’ll need to deprioritize [task] — which should I pause?
Short decline: I can't take this on right now; please try another process or I can revisit on [date].
Urgent channel request: if this is urgent, please mark it as urgent in [channel] and I’ll respond within [time window].
One-liner policy (status/auto-reply): In focused work 9–11 AM. For non-urgent items I will respond by [time].

Limit meetings to specific blocks if possible
Inquire about school run constraints (8:15 weekday) to adjust timing if needed
Commute time: 40 minutes each way during rush hour
Block 15–30 minutes on the calendar as non-negotiable for workouts.
Keep workouts short: 8–20 minute routines (HIIT, bodyweight, or yoga).
Anchor workouts to existing events: after drop-off on WFH days, or after the commute on office days.
Prep workout clothes and gear the night before.
Have a micro-fallback: 2–5 minutes of movement if a full session won't fit.
Use commute time for mobility or a quick walk.
Protect workouts with a rule: workouts happen unless an urgent family or work emergency.
Three short, reliable workouts are more sustainable than longer sessions when mornings go sideways.
MIT delivery options: paper card or 'Today' phone note for daily tasks.
Sticky note reminders on phone for MIT visibility.
Focus timer usage: built-in timer for focus sprints.
Calendar widget showing blocks on phone.

Provide remaining quick details: which weekdays are office vs WFH, your usual meeting load/timing, whether the 8:15 drop-off is every weekday, which evenings are language classes, and your two top priorities for the week.
